source.title: A dual operator view of habitual behavior reflecting cortical and striatal dynamics

confidence_rationale: Strong primary electrophysiology paper. Recordings during overtraining show striatum acquires action-bracketing chunking patterns rapidly while infralimbic cortex develops habit-tracking patterns slowly; optogenetic disruption of infralimbic activity during overtraining prevents habit crystallisation. Direct R2 anchor for striatum-as-behavioural-pattern-extraction substrate (the bottom-up rule discovery analog) plus R4 input on which arbitration variable matters (practice-length, not uncertainty alone).
failure_signatures: An REE substrate without a striatum-analog action-chunking mechanism will fail Smith & Graybiel 2013's signature: rapid acquisition of action-bracketing patterns during overtraining, with sensitivity to running automaticity but insensitivity to reward devaluation.

If ARC-064's bottom-up rule extraction does not develop a stable substrate-level signature over training (in REE: stable rule-cluster representations after extended episodes in similar contexts), the substrate is not 'crystallising' rules the way the dorsolateral striatum does in Smith & Graybiel.

If MECH-312's arbitration variable is purely uncertainty-based (Daw 2005), it will fail to capture the practice-length-driven habit takeover Smith & Graybiel demonstrate; arbitration may need to track time-on-task / repetition-count as well.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Simultaneous recordings in dorsolateral striatum and infralimbic cortex during rat overtraining show that striatum acquires an action-bracketing chunking pattern (signalling start and end of habituated action sequences) rapidly during early training, while infralimbic cortex develops habit-tracking patterns slowly during late overtraining. Optogenetic disruption of infralimbic activity during overtraining prevents habit crystallisation. The two structures act as 'dual operators': striatum compresses behavioural patterns into chunked sequences, infralimbic cortex stabilises the chunks into habits.
mapping.ree_translation: ARC-064's bottom-up rule discovery has a direct biological analog in the dorsolateral striatum's action-chunking pattern: extracting behavioural patterns from the trajectory record and compressing them into reusable chunks. The infralimbic-cortex role (slow stabilisation of the chunks into habit-rules) maps onto the rule-application substrate in REE -- analogous to ARC-062's gated-policy heads. The two-stage architecture (fast extraction in striatum-analog -> slow consolidation in cortex-analog) is a strong R2 verdict on the cluster shape: ARC-064 substrate (extraction) feeds ARC-062 substrate (application) over training time.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Smith & Graybiel's recordings are in rat dorsolateral striatum and infralimbic cortex during a maze task. The chunking signature is action-sequence-level (bracketing the start and end of a stereotyped sequence) rather than rule-level. Mapping to REE's bottom-up rule discovery requires extending the principle from 'stereotyped action chunks' to 'extracted behavioural rules' -- a conceptual scaling-up that is plausible but not directly tested. Transfer risk moderate.
mapping.source_context: Neuron paper. Smith and Graybiel labs (MIT). Foundational primary paper for the action-chunking-in-striatum literature; cited >500 times.

confidence_components.notes: Neuron venue + landmark primary electrophysiology + optogenetic causal manipulation. Mapping fidelity moderate-high; the chunking concept extends naturally to bottom-up rule discovery but is one level smaller than rules in the abstract. Transfer risk moderate.
